title: GDPR Transparency Notice Completeness and Version Alignment

dataSources: Synthetic check results (`index=synthetic`), `gdpr_notice_registry.csv` (site_id, notice_url, required_version), CI/CD web deploy events (Splunkbase 1621 — Splunk CIM Add-on)
app: Splunk Synthetic Monitoring, HTTP Event Collector (HEC)
spl:
index=synthetic check_type="http" earliest=-24h
| lookup gdpr_notice_registry.csv site_id OUTPUT notice_url, required_version
| eval ok=if(http_status=200 AND match(_raw, required_version),1,0)
| where ok=0
| stats latest(http_status) as http_status latest(_time) as last_fail by site_id notice_url
| join site_id [
    search index=cicd sourcetype="web:deploy" earliest=-7d
    | stats latest(release_tag) as release_tag by site_id
]
| table site_id, notice_url, http_status, release_tag, last_fail
description: Transparency requires that data subjects receive fair processing information at collection. This use case monitors CMS and web deployments for privacy notice URL availability, version tags, and broken links — catching releases that ship without updated notices after processing changes.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes cicd, synthetic and sourcetypes web:deploy. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Register each public site with expected notice URL and embedded version string; (2) run synthetic checks every hour from multiple regions; (3) alert privacy counsel when checks fail or version mismatches after deploy; (4) gate production deploys on successful synthetic in mature pipelines; (5) archive passing runs as Art. 12 evidence. Indexes required: `index=cicd`, `index=synthetic`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=web:deploy`; Lookups: `gdpr_notice_registry`, `gdpr_notice_registry.csv`.
visualization: Table (failing sites), Single value (failed checks), Timeline (availability), Bar chart (failures by region). Key fields: `http_status`, `last_fail`, `notice_url`, `ok`, `release_tag`, `site_id`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Privacy notice version transition** — During website updates, brief periods may serve the previous privacy notice version from CDN cache while the new version propagates.

2. **Multi-language translation timing** — Privacy notice updates in the primary language may deploy before all translations are complete, showing version mismatches across language variants.

3. **Acquired entity integration** — Newly acquired business units maintaining separate privacy notices during integration may show discrepancies until consolidated into the group notice.

4. **Embedded third-party notices** — Third-party services embedded in the organisation's platform (payment processors, analytics) maintain their own privacy notices with independent update cycles.

5. **Dark pattern audit false triggers** — Transparency checks that flag consent mechanisms as potentially deceptive may need manual review to distinguish between dark patterns and legitimate UX design choices.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `synthetic`, `cicd` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_notice_registry.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_notice_registry.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.12`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.12 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.50: GDPR Transparency Notice Completeness and Version Alignment." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `synthetic`, `cicd` under `web:deploy`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Register each public site with expected notice URL and embedded version string. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 2:** run synthetic checks every hour from multiple regions. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert privacy counsel when checks fail or version mismatches after deploy. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** gate production deploys on successful synthetic in mature pipelines. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** archive passing runs as Art. 12 evidence. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Transparency Notice Completeness and Version Alignment," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates `index=synthetic` (sourcetype=`web:deploy`) on the outer side with `index=cicd` (sourcetype=`another`) on the inner side, joined on `site_id` across the last 24 hours. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `site_id` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.50: GDPR Transparency Notice Completeness and Version Alignment]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.50,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=synthetic sourcetype=web:deploy earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `http_status`, `last_fail`, `notice_url`, `ok`, `release_tag`, `site_id`. Run:

```spl
index=synthetic sourcetype=web:deploy earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table http_status, last_fail, notice_url, ok, release_tag, site_id
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_notice_registry.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_notice_registry.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.50: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`failing sites`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`failed checks`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Time trend (`availability`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 4 — Distribution (`failures by region`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We transparency requires that data subjects receive fair processing information at collection so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Publish a test privacy notice update on the primary website while leaving the mobile app and third-party integrations showing the previous version for 7 days; verify the completeness check identifies the version mismatch.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Publish a test privacy notice update simultaneously across all channels (web, mobile, third-party) within 24 hours; verify no version alignment alert fires and all touchpoints show consistent notice versions.

evidence: Transparency notice alignment report showing current notice version across all channels, discrepancies detected, resolution timeline, and publication confirmation evidence.
exclusions: Third-party embedded service privacy notices maintained independently by the third party; acquired entity notices during documented integration transition periods.
